summary: Joshua E. Wilson served as an early principal of the Colored Graded School in Florence, South Carolina, established in 1866 by the Freedmen’s Bureau as a school for Black children. His leadership helped shape one of the city’s first institutions dedicated to African American education during Reconstruction, a period of fragile progress before the imposition of Jim Crow laws.

historicalContext: After the Civil War, the Freedmen’s Bureau supported the creation of schools for newly freed African Americans, recognizing education as central to citizenship and autonomy. In South Carolina, where Black people briefly gained political and civil rights during Reconstruction, schools like the Colored Graded School in Florence became vital community institutions—spaces of resistance and resilience amid rising white supremacist backlash.

summary: Jacob E. Freeman served in the Texas House of Representatives during the Fourteenth and Sixteenth Legislatures (1874, 1880), representing Waller, Fort Bend, and Wharton counties. His tenure occurred during Reconstruction, a period of expanded political participation for African Americans in the post-Civil War South.

claims.object: Waller, Fort Bend, and Wharton counties

historicalContext: During Reconstruction, the 14th and 15th Amendments enabled Black men to vote and hold office across the South. Freeman’s election reflected broader efforts by newly enfranchised African Americans to shape state governments, even as white supremacist resistance grew. Texas saw dozens of Black legislators elected during this brief window before Jim Crow laws dismantled these gains.

summary: Jeremiah J. Hamilton, born enslaved in 1838, became a Republican legislator in Texas during Reconstruction, representing Fayette and Bastrop counties from 1870 to 1873. He founded a school for Black students in Bastrop in 1866 and served as secretary of the Texas State Central Committee of Colored Men, advocating for civil rights and political inclusion.

summary: Robert A. Kerr, born in New Orleans and enslaved by his father, became a Reconstruction-era legislator in Texas, elected in 1880 as a Greenback Party representative from Bastrop County. He opposed the convict lease system and helped establish the first high school for African Americans in his county, contributing to Black civic institution-building during a period of resurgent white supremacy.

historicalContext: Kerr’s election occurred during Reconstruction, a brief window of Black political participation after the Civil War, when federal enforcement of civil rights enabled African Americans to hold office in Southern states. His role in opposing the convict lease system—a practice that criminalized Black laborers and perpetuated forced labor—places him within broader struggles against post-slavery racial control mechanisms in the South.

summary: Doc C. Lewis, a Black Republican farmer from Wharton County, served in the Texas House of Representatives from 1881 to 1883 during the Seventeenth Legislature. He was one of four African American members at the time, representing a rare period of Black political participation in Texas during Reconstruction before disenfranchisement intensified.

summary: Sheppard Mullens served in the Texas House of Representatives and as a McLennan County commissioner during Reconstruction, part of a generation of Black officials elected after federal enforcement enabled Black male suffrage. His tenure reflects the brief expansion of political power for African Americans in the postwar South before Jim Crow restrictions.

As a delegate to Texas' 1868 Constitutional Convention and later legislator, he participated in a transformative but short-lived era of biracial democracy enforced by federal authority.

summary: Alonzo Sledge served as a Republican member of the Texas House of Representatives from 1879 to 1881, representing Washington and Burleson counties during the Reconstruction era. As one of the African American legislators elected in the post-Civil War period, his service reflects the brief expansion of Black political power in the South before the rise of Jim Crow laws restricted it.

summary: Henry Sneed, formerly enslaved, was elected to the Texas House of Representatives in 1876, representing Waller, Wharton, and Fort Bend counties during Reconstruction. He was one of three African Americans in the 15th Texas Legislature, illustrating Black political engagement amid postwar societal transformation.

summary: James H. Stewart, born in Louisiana and later of Hearne, Texas, served in the Texas House of Representatives from 1885 to 1887 as a Republican representing the 48th District. A graduate of Prairie View State Normal School, he was among the first Black legislators in Texas during Reconstruction, contributing to early efforts in Black political representation after Emancipation.

summary: James H. Washington, born in Virginia and educated at Oberlin, served as a Republican representative for Grimes County in the Texas House during the 1873 Thirteenth Legislature, following a contested election. He later served on the Galveston city council and as a customs inspector, contributing to Black political leadership during Reconstruction before systemic disfranchisement.

summary: George W. Wyatt, an African American Republican and teacher from Hempstead, Texas, served in the Texas House of Representatives from 1883 to 1885 during the Eighteenth Legislature. He was one of only two Black members in the chamber at the time, representing Waller and Fort Bend counties during Reconstruction's waning years of Black political engagement.
historicalContext: Wyatt’s election reflects the brief expansion of Black political power in the post-Civil War South under Reconstruction, when Black men gained suffrage and held office despite rising white resistance. His service occurred just before the systematic disenfranchisement of Black voters through poll taxes, literacy tests, and violence that dismantled multiracial democracy in Texas by the early 20th century.

summary: Stephen Bates, born into slavery at Shirley Plantation, Virginia, became the first Black sheriff in Vermont history when elected in Vergennes in 1879. He served 25 years as sheriff and police chief, winning repeated elections in a small, predominantly white city, and was recognized with a state commemorative day in 2021.
locationLabel: Vergennes City Park and former sheriff's jurisdiction

historicalContext: Bates’s election as sheriff in 1879 occurred during a period of retrenchment in Black civil rights nationally, making his sustained political success in a Northern, non-urban setting exceptional. His trajectory—from enslavement to elected office—reflects both the possibilities of Reconstruction-era political inclusion and the localized nature of Black leadership in rural New England communities.

summary: Briton Baskerville, Jr. (1863–1892), born into slavery in Mecklenburg County, Virginia, served in the Virginia House of Delegates from 1887 to 1888 as a Republican. A graduate of Boydton Academic and Bible Institute and Wayland Institute, he was a schoolteacher and Sunday school superintendent at Bloom Hill Baptist Church.

historicalContext: Baskerville’s election occurred during the waning years of Reconstruction-era Black political representation in Virginia, when African Americans briefly gained access to state legislatures before being systematically excluded through poll taxes, literacy tests, and violence. His service reflects both the possibilities of emancipation and the fragility of those gains in the face of resurgent white Democratic power.

summary: Peter J. Carter, born enslaved in Northampton County, Virginia, became one of the first African Americans elected to the Virginia House of Delegates (1871–1879). A Union Army veteran of the 10th U.S. Colored Infantry, he advocated for civil rights, prison reform, and equitable jury representation, and helped lead a delegation to President Grant supporting the Civil Rights Act of 1875.

summary: Miles Connor (c. 1830s–1893), born enslaved in Norfolk County, Virginia, became a community leader and Republican officeholder during Reconstruction. He served in the Virginia House of Delegates (1875–1877), held local offices including justice of the peace, and co-founded Churchland Grove Baptist Church. His son, Miles Washington Connor, became the first president of Coppin State University.
